top_findings.finding: Screen reader user could not access table cell content (ticks, crosses, yes/no indicators) in a comparison table, rendering the table unable to communicate plan inclusion information.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant navigated the table using screen reader commands (blend row, column navigation), encountered blank cells, scrolled to the bottom, and confirmed no accessible content was available.
top_findings.stated_signal: All the cells are blank, so it's not really giving me any information at all. My screen reader doesn't seem to be picking any of that up. There's no nothing that my screen reader can access there. So yeah, that table isn't really helpful for me to make any kind of decision or know what each plan includes.
top_findings.rationale: Both observed behaviour and stated utterance confirm that the screen reader failed to expose table cell content (likely visual indicators such as checkmarks or text). The participant explicitly stated the table was unhelpful for decision-making, indicating a component task (understanding plan comparison) was blocked by accessibility failure. This is a classic F3 accessibility barrier where visual-only content was not programmatically available to assistive technology.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user cohort experienced complete information loss due to inaccessible table markup. Sighted users could likely read visual indicators (ticks, crosses, yes/no), but assistive technology users received no equivalent semantic content.

top_findings.finding: Screen reader user could not locate table content on the health assessments page despite using standard screen reader navigation commands.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant pressed 'T' to navigate to tables and received 'There are no tables on this page' response twice; then attempted 'H' to navigate headings and eventually found a heading but not the expected table structure.
top_findings.stated_signal: There are no tables on this page. Hm. Let me try again. There are no tables on this page. OK, see there's no tables on this page, but this is specifically about looking at the information in a table.
top_findings.rationale: The participant's task was to examine health assessment information in table format, but the screen reader did not recognize any table structure on the page, blocking the intended task completion pathway. The participant's statement confirms the mismatch between task expectation (table-based information) and what the assistive technology could access. This represents a complete task component blocker.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user relying on semantic table markup encountered a page where table structure is not properly marked up or is absent, preventing access to the primary task pathway.

top_findings.finding: Participant using keyboard link navigation (Insert+7) could not locate the 'sponsorship of Paralympics' link and cycled through an unrelated set of links starting with 'S' without finding the target.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant activated the links list dialogue, pressed 'S' to filter links, and cycled through 'symptoms', 'sign in or create account', 'support', and other 'S' links without encountering 'sponsorship of Paralympics'.
top_findings.stated_signal: So I'm looking for sponsorship of Paralympics. So I'm assuming that will be a link.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated the search target and used keyboard navigation to attempt to locate it, but the required link was not found in the filtered list. The observed cycling through multiple unrelated links combined with the stated search intent indicates the information pathway was not accessible via the expected navigation method, representing a significant barrier that the participant did not fully resolve in this window.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Keyboard navigation user (likely blind or low-vision) experienced structural amplification: link list filtering by first letter ('S') surfaced many irrelevant results, making efficient keyboard-based discovery of the specific 'sponsorship of Paralympics' link difficult or impossible without additional browsing or alternative pathways.

top_findings.finding: Participant using screen reader could not locate a video on the page and had to rely on transcripts as an alternative.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant pressed F to navigate form controls, searched the page, and concluded the video was not accessible or present, then shifted to using the transcript.
top_findings.stated_signal: I can't seem to see a video on this page, but it's got transcripts. If the video was accessible as well, if there is a video, I'm not sure if there is.
top_findings.rationale: The participant actively searched for a video using keyboard navigation (pressing F for form controls) but could not find it. The stated uncertainty combined with the failed search indicates the video content was not discoverable via assistive technology, requiring the participant to work around the barrier by using transcripts instead. This is a significant component failure that was skipped rather than resolved.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user experiencing amplified friction due to video not being properly labeled, announced, or accessible via keyboard navigation. The video discovery pathway appears invisible to assistive technology.

top_findings.finding: Screen reader user could not access content in a table column and stated the table was not helpful for making plan decisions.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant used screen reader to navigate a dental plan comparison table and encountered a blank column with no accessible content.
top_findings.stated_signal: there's no nothing that my screen reader can can access there. So yeah, that table isn't really helpful for me to make any kind of decision or know what each plan includes.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly reported that the screen reader could not access table content (stated) and observed that they could not navigate the blank column, preventing them from understanding plan options. This is a significant accessibility barrier that blocked a key component of the task (comparing plans) even though the participant moved on.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user encountered completely inaccessible table column, which is a structural barrier for assistive-technology reliance.

coaching_recommendations.title: Prioritise task-blocking friction before general coaching
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 96 finding-level issue(s), including task-blocking or near-blocking severity (S2).
advice: Review the highest-severity findings before applying general coaching templates.
Prioritise issues that prevented or substantially disrupted task completion.
Use the highest-severity findings to guide focused follow-up coaching.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S2=2, S3=5, S4=25, S5=64. Representative findings: Screen reader user could not access table cell content (ticks, crosses, yes/no indicators) in a comparison table, rendering the table unable to communicate plan inclusion information. | Screen reader user could not locate table content on the health assessments page despite using standard screen reader navigation commands. | Participant using keyboard link navigation (Insert+7) could not locate the 'sponsorship of Paralympics' link and cycled through an unrelated set of links starting with 'S' without finding the target.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 7 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 96 findings spanning 7 distinct friction types (F1,F2,F3,F5,F4,F6,F7). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F1 (Comprehension) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F2 Confidence, F3 Accessibility) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=96 findings across 7 distinct friction types (F1=33, F2=19, F3=19, F5=10, F4=9, F6=5, F7=1).
